references.title: Utilizing early engagement and machine learning to predict student outcomes

extracted.Student Performance Definition: At-risk of Failing in the Program
extracted.Target: Pass, Fail, Conditional Fail, Repeat the Year, Repeat Semester
extracted.Courses:
Train: All Undergraduate Programs at Bangor University in 2016/2017

Test: All Undergraduate Programs at Bangor University in 2015/2016
extracted.Features: [Engagement Ratio, Bangor Engagement Metric, Exam Attendance, 3 Biological Variables, Program Information]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Authors define a metric for attendance called Engagement Ratio and a second called Bangor Engagement Metric which are collected every week - using the attendance data from each of the 12 weeks of the semester. Also, there are 6 Exam Attendance BEMs.

Feature Selection is done using SFS with a 1-NN algorithm first, followed by a LOOCV usage of C4.5 to get a better feature set. 

Experiments are conducted fro every week but authors find that by the 3rd week of the Fall Semester there is enough predictive accuracy for the model.
extracted.Models:
Random Tree,
Random Forest, 
Rotation Forest, MLP, SOM, Naive Bayes, Decision Table, C4.5
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Accuracy: 0.9733 - Random Forest - Pass vs Others Classes Setting}
extracted.Comments:
Well… Predictive modelling work that trains on a dataset from 2016/2017.

Multiple combinations of preprocessings/algorithms are tested, but these involve different model assessment strategies (some use LOO, others use 10-Fold CV).
extracted.Limitations:
Test dataset is the year before.

No consistent model assessment strategy seems to have been used - authors mention LOO, 10-Fold CV and a completely separate test dataset.

Data from a single institution.

Poor choice of metrics for reporting.

annotations.text: Most educators and managers often rely on domain knowledge and intuition to predict students at risk. The paper reviews current methods used in LA and concludse that few approaches actually delve into early prediction. Authors subsequently re-define student attendance AND create a model that, by Week 3 of the Fall Semester achieved an Accuracy of 97%. No specific additional details are provided in the current moment.
